planting.note: Start seed indoors and set out near the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow seed and keep warm and bright until germination, then grow on in full sun. This well-branched seed strain produces abundant white flowers over a mounded plant. Bloom carries from summer to frost in lean, well-drained ground.

planting.note: Start seed indoors; compact habit fits smaller borders.

directions: Sow seed on the surface and keep warm until seedlings emerge, then grow on brightly. This compact seed strain bears soft-pink flowers on a dense, well-branched mound. Flowers appear from summer through fall in full sun and dry soil.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and transplant near the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow seed for light exposure and keep warm and moist until germination. This upright seed strain sends up tall wands of white flowers with pink buds. Bloom runs from early summer to frost in a sunny, well-drained bed.

planting.note: Start seed indoors before the last frost.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and keep warm and bright until sprouts appear. This tall seed strain bears white flowers rimmed with a pink picotee edge on airy stems. Bloom continues from summer to fall in lean, sharply drained soil.

planting.note: Plant potted starts in spring into full sun.

directions: Plant in full sun and gritty, well-drained soil, watering only to establish. This selection produces a generous show of clean white flowers on slender stems. Bloom carries from early summer to frost; shear midseason to refresh.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and set plants out near the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow seed and hold warm and moist until germination, then grow on brightly. This seed strain produces pink flowers on an airy, upright plant. Bloom carries from summer to frost in full sun and lean soil.

planting.note: Start seed indoors before the last spring frost.

directions: Press seed onto the mix surface and keep warm until seedlings emerge. This seed strain carries white flowers on wiry, upright stems that sway in the breeze. Bloom runs from summer to fall in a sunny, well-drained spot.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and transplant out around the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow seed for light and keep warm until germination, then grow on in full sun. This tall seed selection sends up long, airy wands of pink-tinted flowers. Bloom carries from early summer to frost; stake in windy sites if needed.

planting.note: Start seed indoors before the last frost for summer bloom.

directions: Sow seed on the surface and keep warm and lightly moist until sprouts appear. This seed strain bears white flowers on airy, upright stems above narrow foliage. Bloom runs from summer to frost in sunny, sharply drained soil.

planting.note: Plant potted starts in spring into full sun.

directions: Plant this compact selection in full sun with sharp drainage and space for airflow. Soft pink flowers flutter above the foliage on slender stems. Bloom carries from early summer to frost; trim lightly to renew.

planting.note: Plant potted starts in spring into a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant in full sun and lean soil, keeping water modest to avoid floppy growth. This tall form carries pink-flushed flowers on long, graceful wands. Bloom runs from early summer to frost; support may help in exposed sites.

planting.note: Plant potted starts in spring into full sun to keep foliage color bright.

directions: Give this variegated form full sun to hold its cream-and-pink leaf coloring and compact habit. Plant in well-drained soil and water sparingly once established. Pink flowers rise above the colorful foliage from summer to fall.

planting.note: Plant potted starts in spring into full sun for best variegation.

directions: Plant this variegated selection in full sun and sharp drainage to keep the leaf edges bright. Water modestly once the roots take hold. White flowers with a pink blush hover over cream-margined foliage from summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant potted starts after frost into sun; good in containers and borders.

directions: Set plants in full sun and well-drained soil, spacing them for good airflow. This compact selection carries bright pink flowers over a neat, mounded plant. Bloom flushes repeatedly from late spring to fall with little care.

planting.note: Set out divisions or pots in spring once the soil has warmed and settled.

directions: Plant at the same depth the crown sat before, spacing clumps to let the mound knit together. Give it a lean, well-drained spot and shear lightly after the first flush to tidy the foliage. Magenta saucer flowers open in early summer, with scattered rebloom into fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in gritty, free-draining soil.

directions: Tuck this low, spreading form into a rock garden or path edge where drainage is sharp. Trim spent stems after flowering to keep the cushion neat. Pale pink petals veined in deeper rose appear from early to midsummer.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring for a compact, uniform mound.

directions: Because this selection will not come true from seed, start with a nursery division and set the crown level with the soil. Its dense habit needs little care beyond a shearing after the main bloom. Rich magenta flowers cover the mound in summer above finely cut leaves that redden in autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and average soil.

directions: Give this vigorous selection room to sprawl and set the crown at soil level. Shear it back by a third after the first flush to renew the foliage and coax more bloom. Deep purple-magenta flowers open across a long summer season.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in fertile, well-drained ground.

directions: Start from a division since this cultivar does not breed true, and give it rich soil with room to stand tall. Cut it back hard after bloom to freshen the foliage. Pearly gray-blue flowers pencilled with pale veins open in early summer.

planting.note: Propagate only by division and plant in spring.

directions: This sterile double form must be grown from divisions, so start with an established plant and set the crown level. The seedless flowers hold their color for weeks and never self-sow. Fully double violet-purple rosettes crowd the stems in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part shade with steady moisture.

directions: Site this early woodlander in dappled shade or morning sun in cool, humus-rich soil. Deadhead or shear after the main show to keep it tidy. Upward-facing blue-violet flowers with pale centers open in late spring, ahead of most other cranesbills.

planting.note: Plant divisions or rooted stem pieces in spring or fall.

directions: This tough groundcover roots readily from thick surface stems, so press divisions firmly into the soil. It tolerates dry shade and needs almost no care once established, spreading into a weed-smothering mat of aromatic leaves. Magenta-pink flowers appear in late spring, and the foliage colors in fall.

planting.note: Plant rooted divisions in spring or early fall.

directions: Grow from a division and set the fleshy stems just at the soil surface where they will root as they creep. It thrives in dry shade under trees with little attention. Soft pink flowers rise above glossy aromatic leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in sun to shade.

directions: Set divisions with the creeping stems flush to the ground so they take root quickly. This forgiving selection knits into a dense evergreen carpet even in poor, dry soil. Deep magenta flowers with dark red calyces open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in part shade.

directions: Grow from divisions and press the surface stems into soil to establish the mat. It is one of the best groundcovers for dry shade and asks little once rooted. Nearly white blush-pink flowers with pinkish calyces open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in sun or part shade.

directions: This sterile hybrid spreads by rhizomes, so plant divisions and let them run into a low mat. It needs no deadheading and colors nicely in fall. Pale blush-white flowers tinged pink at the edges cover the aromatic foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in average, well-drained soil.

directions: Grow from a division and space plants to knit into a solid groundcover. The dense aromatic foliage suppresses weeds and turns reddish in autumn. Deep carmine-pink flowers blanket the mat in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part to full shade with cool soil.

directions: One of the few cranesbills happy in real shade, this species wants cool, moisture-retentive soil. Cut it to the ground after flowering to renew the leaves. Small nodding maroon-black flowers with reflexed petals hover on wiry stems in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in shade for best leaf color.

directions: Grown mainly for foliage, this selection is best from divisions to keep the leaf pattern true. Give it cool shade and steady moisture and cut back tired leaves after bloom. Each green leaf carries a bold chocolate-maroon band, topped by dusky purple flowers in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a shaded border.

directions: This pale form lights up shade and is best divided in spring to keep it uniform. Provide cool, humus-rich soil and shear spent stems after flowering. Pure white nodding flowers float above fresh green leaves in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sun or light shade with average soil.

directions: An easy, weaving species that forms a spreading clump in most soils. Cut it back after the first flush and it reblooms through the season. Bright salmon-pink funnel flowers open from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in sun to part shade.

directions: Grow from a division for a reliable, long-flowering clump that weaves through neighbors. Shear it hard in midsummer to renew the foliage and prompt fresh bloom. Bright salmon-pink flowers carry on from late spring well into fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; vigorous and adaptable.

directions: This robust selection makes a large weed-proof clump and is best planted from divisions. Cut it back in summer to control size and freshen the leaves. Rose-pink flowers with darker veining bloom over a long season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun to light shade.

directions: Plant divisions in fertile, well-drained soil and give them room to spread by short rhizomes. Shear after the main flush for a lighter rebloom. Large violet-blue flowers with a reddish center open in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny border.

directions: A more compact selection best grown from divisions to keep its neat form. Give it good drainage and shear lightly after bloom. Deep violet-blue flowers with a reddish eye appear in early summer on a tidy mound.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun to part shade.

directions: This classic sterile hybrid is grown only from divisions and never sets seed. Cut the whole plant back after flowering to renew the mound of divided leaves. Clear blue saucer flowers cover the clump in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions or pots in spring after frost.

directions: This sterile award-winner is propagated by division and blooms nonstop without deadheading. Give it room to sprawl and let it lace through nearby plants. Violet-blue flowers with white centers open from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in sun to light shade.

directions: A tidier sport of Rozanne, this sterile plant is grown from divisions and needs no deadheading. Its more compact habit suits smaller borders and containers. Soft sky-blue flowers with pale centers bloom from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: Grow this vigorous hybrid from divisions and give it fertile soil in full sun. Cut it back after the first flush to keep the finely cut foliage fresh and prompt rebloom. Deep blue flowers with small white eyes open through early and midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny, open spot.

directions: This large, free-flowering hybrid is grown from divisions and makes a bold clump. Give it room and shear back after the heaviest flush to tidy it. Big violet-blue flowers with reddish veining bloom over a long summer season.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun.

directions: A vigorous psilostemon hybrid grown from divisions that forms a large mound. Cut it back after the main flush to control size and encourage rebloom. Bright magenta flowers with black centers glow from early summer onward.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; give it space to ramble.

directions: This sprawling sterile hybrid is grown from divisions and threads its long stems through nearby plants. Give it room and let it scramble rather than staking it. Magenta flowers with black centers open against yellow-green young foliage all summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun to part shade.

directions: A sterile, long-blooming hybrid grown from divisions that spreads widely without seeding. Let it weave through the border and shear it if it overreaches. Large magenta flowers with dark centers open continuously through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile soil with full sun.

directions: This tall species makes an imposing clump and may need support in rich soil. Cut it to the ground after flowering for a flush of fresh leaves. Brilliant magenta flowers with striking black centers open in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Give this clumping species full sun and average, well-drained soil. Shear it after the main bloom to keep the mound of broad leaves tidy. Deep violet-blue flowers with fine dark veins open densely in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in sun to part shade.

directions: Grow from divisions and give this spreader room to run by rhizomes. Cut it back after flowering to renew the finely cut leaves. Large lilac-purple flowers with pale veining open in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a lightly shaded border.

directions: This spreading selection is grown from divisions and roams gently on rhizomes. Give it moisture-retentive soil and cut back after bloom. White flowers delicately penciled with pink-lilac veins open in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; it starts slowly and peaks late.

directions: Give this trailing plant room to scramble through neighbors from a late start in spring. It hits its stride in late summer when many geraniums fade. Sky-blue flowers with distinct white centers open from midsummer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Grow this compact species in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun for the best foliage. It dislikes wet feet, so avoid heavy clay. Its sage-green, velvety scalloped leaves are topped by white flowers boldly veined in violet in early summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a rock garden or trough.

directions: This tiny alpine hybrid is grown from divisions and wants sharp drainage in a sunny raised spot. Keep it lean and avoid winter wet. Pale lilac-pink flowers heavily veined in maroon with dark eyes bloom over a long summer above gray-green leaves.

planting.note: Plant in spring in gritty soil in sun to light shade.

directions: This dwarf spreader creeps slowly into a tidy mat, ideal for edging or a rock garden. Give it good drainage and it needs almost no care. Shell-pink flowers open above glossy aromatic leaves that redden in fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade; tolerates dry ground.

directions: One of the best cranesbills for dry shade, it self-sows modestly and fills gaps under trees. Cut it back if it looks tired for a fresh flush. Glossy leaves set off lilac-pink funnel flowers from late spring into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring in woodland soil; seed needs cold stratifying.

directions: This North American native thrives in dappled woodland with humus-rich, moist soil. Let it self-sow to form a natural drift and cut back after flowering. Soft lavender-pink flowers open in mid to late spring for pollinators.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in part shade for leaf color.

directions: Grown for its foliage, this selection is best from divisions and colors best with some sun. Give it moist woodland soil and cut back after bloom. Chocolate-brown leaves contrast with pale pink flowers in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in sun to part shade.

directions: This white-flowered hybrid spreads by rhizomes and is grown from divisions into a weed-proof mat. It needs no deadheading and colors up in fall. White flowers with a faint pink flush cover the aromatic foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; give it wide spacing.

directions: A vigorous, wide-spreading hybrid grown from divisions that quickly covers ground. Cut it back hard after the first flush to refresh it and prompt rebloom. Bright magenta-pink flowers appear in profusion from early summer on.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in full sun to part shade.

directions: This compact, mounding hybrid is grown from divisions and blooms over a long season without deadheading. Its low spreading habit suits the front of a border. Soft pale-pink flowers with darker veins open from early summer to fall.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or set out divisions after the ground thaws.

directions: Sow seed barely covered and keep the surface evenly moist until sprouts appear in two to four weeks. Give the plant humus-rich soil that never fully dries out and shear spent stems to prompt a second flush. Bright orange-red single flowers open above the mounded foliage in late spring.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter; move seedlings out once frost danger passes.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and keep warm and moist until germination. Grow in fertile, well-drained soil and deadhead often to extend the display. This species is the parent of many garden hybrids and carries bold scarlet blooms on branching stems in early summer.

planting.note: Best where soil stays damp; a cold spell before sowing improves sprouting.

directions: Sow seed after a period of cold, moist chilling and keep the tray damp throughout germination. Plant along a pond edge or in a boggy border where the roots never bake dry. Nodding, dusky pink and cream bell-shaped flowers dangle from reddish stems in mid to late spring.

planting.note: A rock-garden species; give sharp drainage and cool roots.

directions: Sow chilled seed in a gritty mix and keep cool while it germinates over several weeks. Plant in a raised bed or trough with excellent drainage, mimicking its native mountain scree. Large golden-yellow single flowers sit low over the tidy foliage in late spring, followed by fluffy seedheads.

planting.note: A hardy meadow species; direct-sow in spring or fall.

directions: Direct-sow onto raked soil and firm the seed in gently. This widespread species adapts to a range of soils in sun or light shade and needs little care once established. Upright stems carry small bright yellow flowers in early summer, followed by clinging hooked seedheads.

planting.note: A moisture-loving woodland native; sow where soil stays damp.

directions: Scatter seed in moist, humus-rich ground in spring and keep it damp until seedlings appear. This vigorous species suits naturalistic and streamside plantings in sun to partial shade. Clusters of small yellow flowers open in late spring above the broad basal leaves that give it its name.

planting.note: An alpine species that spreads by runners; give gritty, fast-draining soil.

directions: Sow cold-treated seed in a sharply drained scree mix and keep cool during germination. Plant in a rock garden where its red runners can root and form a low mat. Large yellow flowers appear just above the foliage in summer, followed by silky seed plumes.

planting.note: A sterile hybrid; increase by division rather than seed.

directions: Set divisions with the crown level with the soil in fertile, well-drained ground. Because it is sterile it channels energy into a very long bloom season, so deadhead lightly to keep it going. Airy stems hold soft apricot-orange single flowers from late spring into summer.

planting.note: This old strain comes fairly true from seed started indoors in late winter.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep it warm and moist for two to four weeks. Grow in rich, well-drained soil and remove faded stems to encourage repeat bloom. Semi-double, brilliant scarlet flowers rise on branching stems from late spring into summer.

planting.note: A classic yellow counterpart to 'Mrs J Bradshaw', grown much the same way.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and hold at warm temperatures until it germinates. Give it fertile soil and steady moisture, deadheading through the season for more flowers. Semi-double, golden-yellow blooms appear on wiry stems from late spring onward.

planting.note: Increase this selection by dividing established clumps in spring or fall.

directions: Plant divisions in humus-rich, moisture-retentive soil with the crown at surface level. Deadhead spent flowers to prolong the compact display and divide every few years to keep it vigorous. Vivid orange single flowers cover the low mound in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: A named hybrid from the Cocktails series; propagate by division.

directions: Set divisions in well-drained soil enriched with compost and water them in well. Deadhead regularly to keep the plant blooming and divide clumps every few years to maintain vigor. Semi-double flowers open in muted peach and apricot tones over an unusually long season.

planting.note: A Cocktails-series hybrid best increased by division.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown at soil level in fertile, evenly moist ground. Cut back spent flower stems to prompt a fresh round of bloom through the season. Warm yellow petals brushed with orange-red edges give the flowers their sunrise coloring in early summer.

planting.note: A compact hybrid; lift and divide clumps to multiply.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained soil and keep them watered until established. Deadhead to extend flowering and refresh crowded clumps by dividing every few years. Ruffled semi-double flowers in fiery scarlet-orange bloom on compact plants from late spring.

planting.note: A fully double red selection; propagate by division.

directions: Set divisions in rich, well-drained soil with the crown level with the surface. Remove faded blooms to keep new buds forming and divide every third year for best flowering. Fully double, bright scarlet flowers resemble tiny roses on upright stems in early summer.

planting.note: A compact orange selection increased by division.

directions: Plant divisions in moisture-retentive, well-drained soil and water them in. Deadhead regularly and lift crowded clumps to keep the plant flowering freely. Bright tangerine-orange single flowers dance above the low green mound from late spring into summer.

planting.note: A Cocktails-series hybrid; divide to propagate.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown at soil level in fertile ground with good drainage. Deadhead spent stems to keep the long bloom season going and divide clumps periodically. Semi-double flowers open cream flushed with soft pink at the ruffled petal edges.

planting.note: A Cocktails-series selection best increased by division.

directions: Set divisions into compost-enriched, well-drained soil and firm them gently. Trim away finished flowers to prompt continued bloom and divide crowded plants in spring. Semi-double blooms blend warm yellow and orange with rosy pink edges over a long season.

planting.note: A compact modern hybrid propagated by division.

directions: Plant divisions level with the soil in fertile, well-drained ground and water in well. Its tidy habit needs little staking; deadhead to keep flowers coming. Semi-double peach-pink flowers appear over neat foliage from late spring into summer.

planting.note: A long-flowering hybrid increased by division.

directions: Set divisions in humus-rich, well-drained soil with the crown at surface level. Remove spent stems regularly to sustain the extended bloom period and divide every few years. Large semi-double flowers in glowing scarlet-orange open above the foliage from late spring.

planting.note: A semi-double red hybrid propagated by division.

directions: Plant divisions in fertile, moisture-retentive soil that drains freely and water them in. Deadhead to keep the flowers coming and lift crowded clumps to renew them. Nodding, semi-double deep red flowers hang from dark stems from late spring into summer.

planting.note: A selected form of water avens; divide to keep it true.

directions: Plant divisions in reliably moist, humus-rich soil in sun or dappled shade. Ideal at a pond margin or in a damp border where it will slowly form a clump. Nodding, coppery-pink bell-shaped flowers appear on arching stems in mid to late spring.

planting.note: A Cocktails-series hybrid best increased by division.

directions: Set divisions with the crown at soil level in fertile, well-drained ground. Deadhead through the season to sustain flowering and divide clumps every few years. Semi-double flowers in warm orange and gold with reddish tones bloom over a long spell in early summer.

planting.note: A soft-yellow Cocktails-series hybrid propagated by division.

directions: Plant divisions in compost-enriched, well-drained soil and water them in thoroughly. Remove faded flowers to prolong the bloom and divide crowded clumps in spring. Semi-double, soft creamy-yellow flowers open above tidy foliage from late spring into summer.

planting.note: An older orange selection; divide to propagate.

directions: Plant divisions in fertile, well-drained soil with the crown level with the surface. Deadhead spent stems to encourage repeat flowering through the season. Semi-double, deep orange flowers rise on branching stems in early summer.

planting.note: A compact early hybrid best increased by division.

directions: Plant divisions in moisture-retentive, well-drained soil in sun or light shade. Deadhead to lengthen the display and divide clumps every few years to keep them vigorous. Nodding, warm orange-yellow flowers appear early over the low green mound in spring.

planting.note: A pale-flowered water avens selection; divide to keep it true.

directions: Plant divisions in consistently damp, humus-rich soil in partial shade. Well suited to a bog garden or streamside where the roots stay cool and moist. Nodding, pale lemon-yellow bell flowers dangle from reddish calyces in late spring.

planting.note: A double-flowered red hybrid increased by division.

directions: Set divisions in rich, well-drained soil and water them in until established. Remove spent flowers to keep new buds forming and divide every few years for vigor. Fully double, glowing scarlet flowers cover the plant in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Grown for chartreuse foliage as well as flowers; divide to propagate.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained soil, siting in light afternoon shade to protect the bright foliage from scorch. Deadhead to prolong bloom and enjoy the leaves all season. Scarlet-orange single flowers contrast against the lime-green leaves in late spring.

planting.note: An older orange-red selection increased by division.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown at soil level in fertile, well-drained ground. Deadhead spent stems to encourage further bloom and divide clumps periodically. Semi-double, orange-red flowers open on branching stems in early summer.

planting.note: A pink water avens selection; divide to keep it true.

directions: Plant divisions in damp, humus-rich soil in sun or partial shade. Give it a moist border or waterside spot where the clump can slowly spread. Nodding, warm pink double flowers hang from arching stems in mid to late spring.

planting.note: An orange semi-double hybrid propagated by division.

directions: Set divisions in fertile, well-drained soil and water them in thoroughly. Deadhead to extend the bloom and lift crowded clumps every few years to renew them. Semi-double, ruffled orange flowers appear on upright stems in early summer.

planting.note: An old orange semi-double selection increased by division.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown level with the soil in rich, well-drained ground. Deadhead regularly to keep the long display going and divide clumps every few years. Semi-double, glowing orange flowers rise on branching stems from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Sow outdoors once the soil warms after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow seed about one-eighth inch deep in gritty, sharply drained neutral to alkaline soil and keep barely moist until sprouts appear. Give the fleshy taproot a permanent spot since it resents transplanting once established. Expect the airy cloud of tiny white flowers to peak in early to midsummer, often reblooming if sheared back.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants after frost danger passes in spring.

directions: Plant this fully double white selection at the same depth it grew in its pot, in lean alkaline soil with excellent drainage. Because the double flowers set little seed, propagate only from stem cuttings or buy grafted stock rather than expecting seedlings. Shear spent stems in midsummer to encourage a lighter second flush of the frothy white bloom.

planting.note: Transplant nursery-grown plants in spring after the ground warms.

directions: This selection carries larger double white flowers than the older Bristol Fairy on a robust upright plant. Give it full sun and gritty, alkaline soil, and stake or ring the billowing stems if the site is windy. Cut long sprays for fresh or dried arrangements just as most florets open in summer.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: A tidy dwarf form smothered in small double white flowers, well suited to the front of a border or a large container. Plant in sharply drained soil and avoid wet winter conditions that rot the crown. Trim lightly after the first flush to keep the mound neat and coax more bloom.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants in spring once frost has passed.

directions: This tall selection produces clouds of soft double pink flowers on strong stems. Plant in full sun and lean alkaline soil, allowing plenty of room for the wide, airy habit. Support the plant early in the season and harvest sprays for cutting when the pink florets are freshly opened.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into a sunny, sharply drained spot.

directions: A compact double pink form that blooms over a long stretch of summer without flopping. Give it gritty, alkaline soil and full sun, and keep the crown dry through winter. Shear back faded flowers to encourage a repeat show of the soft pink haze.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock after the last spring frost.

directions: Also sold as Snowflake, this vigorous cultivar bears masses of small semi-double to double white flowers. Set plants in full sun in lean, well-drained alkaline soil and avoid overfeeding, which reduces flowering. Cut generous sprays in summer for bouquets, where they last well both fresh and dried.

planting.note: Plant out in spring after frost into full sun.

directions: Part of the dwarf Festival group, this cultivar forms a low mound of tiny white double flowers ideal for edging and pots. Plant in gritty, well-drained soil and full sun for the tidiest habit. Deadhead or lightly shear after blooming to prompt a second flush later in summer.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants once spring frosts have ended.

directions: A soft pink dwarf selection in the Festival series, compact enough for containers and border fronts. Give it full sun and sharply drained soil, keeping the roots on the dry side. Shear off spent bloom to keep the mound compact and encourage further flowering.

planting.note: Transplant in spring into a sunny, well-drained location.

directions: A low, spreading selection that produces a long-lasting cloud of small double white flowers. Plant in lean, alkaline, fast-draining soil and full sun for best performance. Its compact habit rarely needs staking, and light shearing keeps flowers coming through the season.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring after the danger of frost.

directions: A florist favorite grown for its dense sprays of tiny white flowers on wiry stems. Set plants in full sun and sharply drained alkaline soil, avoiding heavy feeding. Cut stems for the vase when most florets have opened, as they dry and hold color exceptionally well.

planting.note: Plant out in spring once soils have warmed.

directions: A widely grown cut-flower cultivar prized for uniform, long, well-branched stems of small white blooms. Grow in full sun and lean, well-drained soil, giving each plant room for airflow. Harvest sprays as the flowers open for arrangements that last a long time fresh or dried.

planting.note: Sow directly in the garden around the last spring frost.

directions: Scatter seed thinly and barely cover it, keeping the soil moist until seedlings emerge in a week or two. This fast, short-lived annual blooms quickly, so make repeat sowings every two to three weeks for continuous flowers. Expect airy sprays of small white blossoms within about six to eight weeks of sowing.

planting.note: Direct sow around the last frost and again in succession.

directions: A classic large-flowered white strain grown for cutting, bearing bigger single blooms than the wild type. Sow shallowly in full sun and thin the seedlings so plants can branch freely. Make successive sowings for a steady supply and cut sprays as the florets open for the vase.

planting.note: Sow in place near the last frost in a sunny bed.

directions: This selection carries rose to carmine-pink single flowers instead of the usual white. Sow seed thinly and just cover it, thinning to give plants space to branch. Repeat sowings extend the display, and stems can be cut for airy pink filler in bouquets.

planting.note: Direct sow at the last frost and resow through early summer.

directions: A soft pink annual strain that flowers quickly from seed in a single season. Barely cover the seed and keep it damp until the seedlings appear, then thin for airflow. Enjoy the pale pink haze within about seven weeks, cutting sprays freely for arrangements.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring in a gritty rock-garden site.

directions: This trailing alpine species forms a low mat that spills nicely over walls and rocks. Plant in sharply drained, gritty alkaline soil in full sun and avoid soggy conditions. Small white to pale pink flowers cover the mat in late spring and early summer, and a light trim afterward keeps it tidy.

planting.note: Plant divisions or pots in spring in a well-drained rockery.

directions: A pink-flowered form of the creeping species that carpets rock gardens and slopes. Set plants in gritty, fast-draining soil in full sun, keeping the crown dry over winter. Shear lightly after the late-spring flush of pink flowers to encourage dense regrowth.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a sunny, sharply drained rockery.

directions: A pure white selection of creeping baby's breath ideal for edging paths and cascading over walls. Grow in gritty alkaline soil in full sun and avoid winter wet. The low mat becomes a sheet of small white flowers in late spring, tidied by a quick shear once bloom fades.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants in spring after frost passes.

directions: A free-flowering creeping selection producing a long show of soft rose-pink blooms. Plant in sharp-draining gritty soil in full sun, giving it room to trail. Shearing after the first heavy flush keeps the mat compact and often triggers repeat flowering.

planting.note: Plant out in spring in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: A long-blooming white creeping form good for containers, edging, and rock gardens. Grow in gritty, sharply drained alkaline soil in full sun. Cut it back lightly after the main flush to maintain a neat mat and encourage further white flowers.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock in spring once frosts have ended.

directions: Also sold as Rosy Veil or Fairy's Veil, this low, spreading hybrid bears clouds of small double flowers that open white and age to pale pink. Plant in full sun and lean, well-drained alkaline soil, allowing room for the wide mounding habit. Shear after the first flush for a long, softly two-toned display through summer.

planting.note: Sow in place near the last frost or start indoors early.

directions: A dainty low annual that forms a spreading cushion covered in tiny pink or white flowers. Barely cover the fine seed and keep it moist until it sprouts, thinning to a hand's width apart. It blooms for weeks in containers and edges, flowering roughly two months after sowing.

planting.note: Start indoors about six weeks before the last frost, then plant out.

directions: An award-winning selection forming a tidy mound smothered in tiny semi-double deep rose flowers. Surface-sow onto warm, moist mix and do not bury the fine seed, which needs light to germinate. Transplant after frost into full sun for months of nonstop bloom in beds, baskets, and pots.

planting.note: Start seed indoors before the last frost and transplant out.

directions: A white counterpart to the popular Gypsy series, forming a compact ball of tiny double flowers. Press the fine seed onto the surface and keep it lit and moist to sprout. Once settled in full sun after frost, it flowers profusely all season in containers and along path edges.

planting.note: Sow indoors weeks ahead of frost, then set out afterward.

directions: A soft pink form of the compact Gypsy series that blooms without pause through the warm months. Surface-sow the tiny seed under bright light and keep the mix evenly damp. Plant into full sun after frost for a rounded cushion of small semi-double flowers in beds and hanging baskets.

planting.note: Start indoors ahead of frost and transplant into full sun.

directions: A trailing selection that mounds and spills, covered in tiny soft pink flowers for months. Sow onto the surface of a warm, moist mix and leave the seed uncovered for light. Move plants outdoors after frost into full sun, where they thrive in baskets and container edges.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring in a gritty alpine trough or rockery.

directions: A tiny Himalayan alpine forming a flat rosette of soft gray-green leaves that hugs the ground. Grow in very well-drained gritty soil in full sun to light shade, ideal for troughs and rock crevices. Relatively large white flowers veined in pink appear in late spring above the low foliage.

planting.note: Establish in a sharply drained trough or scree in spring.

directions: An extremely dense, rock-hard cushion prized by alpine collectors for its buns of minute leaves. Plant in pure grit or a lean scree mix in full sun, keeping it dry, especially in winter. Growth is very slow and flowering sparse, with tiny stemless white blooms nestled in the cushion.

planting.note: Sow in spring in deep, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: A robust perennial species larger and coarser than common baby's breath, forming a broad airy mound. Sow shallowly in deep, sharply drained soil and give the deep root a permanent home. Loose sprays of small pale pink flowers open in mid to late summer, good for cutting.

planting.note: Sow or plant in spring in a hot, dry, well-drained spot.

directions: A tough, heat-tolerant species with blue-green foliage and a long late-season bloom. Plant in lean, sharply drained soil in full sun where it shrugs off drought once established. Clouds of small pink flowers appear from midsummer well into fall, later than most baby's breath.

planting.note: Sow in spring in gritty, well-drained ground in full sun.

directions: A tufted European species of dry, sandy and limestone soils, more modest than common baby's breath. Sow shallowly in lean, sharply drained soil in full sun and avoid rich, wet ground. Narrow gray-green leaves support open sprays of small white to cream flowers in summer.

planting.note: Establish in a gritty rock garden or trough in spring.

directions: A neat cushion-forming alpine from the Caucasus with fine needle-like foliage. Grow in very sharp-draining gritty soil in full sun, keeping it lean and on the dry side. Short stems carry small pink flowers just above the tuft in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock in spring after frost in full sun.

directions: A commercial cut-flower cultivar grown for large, well-branched sprays of small white flowers. Plant in full sun in lean, sharply drained alkaline soil with good airflow. Cut long stems as the florets open, since the sturdy sprays perform well fresh, dried, or dyed.

planting.note: Start seed indoors before frost and transplant out into sun.

directions: A compact white-flowered annual that mounds neatly and blooms all season long. Surface-sow the tiny seed and leave it uncovered for light, keeping the mix evenly moist. Set plants into full sun after frost for weeks of tiny star-like flowers in edges, pots, and baskets.

planting.note: Plant out rooted stock in spring once frost has passed.

directions: A double white selection that produces a dense froth of small pompon flowers on branching stems. Grow in full sun and lean, sharply drained alkaline soil, avoiding heavy fertilizer. Cut sprays for the vase when most florets are open, or shear after bloom for a lighter second flush.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or set out young plants after the last spring frost.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed and press it lightly into moist soil, as light aids germination. Grow in consistently moist, fertile ground and pinch stems back in early summer to keep tall plants sturdy. Golden-yellow daisy blooms with a raised central cone open from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and move plants out after the last frost into moist ground.

directions: Surface-sow seed and firm it gently against moist soil without covering it. This western species thrives in wet meadows and streamside soils, so avoid letting the roots dry out. Yellow petals radiate from a prominent brown dome from early through late summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors after a cold spell, then plant out when frost has passed.

directions: Sow seed on the surface and provide a period of cold to break dormancy for best results. Once known as Helenium hoopesii, this mountain-meadow plant favors well-drained soil and cool summers. Orange-yellow daisies with drooping rays bloom over silvery foliage in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Set out divisions or nursery plants in spring after the ground warms.

directions: Plant divisions at the same depth they grew before and water in well through establishment. This classic cultivar holds up best in moist, rich soil and benefits from division every few years. Coppery-red petals that fade toward burnt orange surround a brown cone from midsummer onward.

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock or divisions in spring.

directions: Set plants into fertile, moisture-retentive soil and keep them watered during dry spells. This selection blooms earlier and longer than most, with a warm blend of orange, red, and gold on each flower. Deadhead regularly and it will flower from early summer until first frost.

planting.note: Plant in spring into warm, workable soil.

directions: Plant divisions or potted stock at their original depth and mulch to retain moisture. This vigorous cultivar produces a festive mix of orange, yellow, and red flowers speckled and streaked across the rays. Blooms carry from midsummer into fall and attract many bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Set out in spring once the soil is workable.

directions: Plant into rich, evenly moist soil and stake the taller stems if the site is windy. Prized for its clear pure-yellow flowers over a golden-brown cone, it gives a bright late-season display. Deadhead spent blooms to extend flowering from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions or nursery plants in spring.

directions: Plant at the original soil depth into fertile ground and keep the roots consistently damp. This German-raised cultivar carries warm golden-orange to coppery flowers on strong upright stems. It blooms from midsummer to early autumn and pairs well with grasses.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring after frost.

directions: Plant into moisture-retentive soil enriched with compost and water well while establishing. The name means ruby dwarf, referring to its deep mahogany-red flowers on comparatively compact stems. Deadhead through the season for a long run of bloom from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring into warm soil.

directions: Plant this dwarf selection into fertile, moist ground toward the front of a border. Its compact habit needs no staking and produces rich red flowers over a long season. Blooms open from early summer to early autumn when deadheaded regularly.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring.

directions: Plant into rich, damp soil and water freely during establishment and dry weather. This early-flowering cultivar bears bright yellow rays around a dark brown-red central boss. It flowers from early summer and rewards regular deadheading with repeat bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant into consistently moist, fertile soil in a sunny spot near the border front. This unusual selection carries semi-double golden-yellow flowers on a tidy, compact plant. It blooms early and long, from early summer into autumn with deadheading.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring into warm soil.

directions: Plant at the original depth and keep the soil evenly moist through summer. Named for the canary, this cultivar shows soft lemon-yellow rays around a greenish-yellow cone. Blooms appear from mid to late summer and hold well for cutting.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring.

directions: Plant into fertile, moisture-holding soil toward the middle of a sunny border. This compact, free-flowering cultivar bears yellow-orange rays streaked and flecked with copper. It blooms early and repeats over a long season if deadheaded.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost has passed.

directions: Set plants into rich, damp soil and water through establishment and dry periods. This cultivar carries bicolored rays of orange-red and gold around a dark central disc. Flowers open from midsummer into early autumn and draw pollinators.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring.

directions: Plant into fertile, evenly moist soil and mulch to conserve moisture in summer. The name translates to flaming Katie, describing its fiery red-and-gold flowers. Blooms run from midsummer to early autumn on strong upright stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring into warm, workable soil.

directions: Plant at the original depth into moist, fertile ground in full sun. This cultivar is noted for narrow, spidery rolled rays in warm yellow and orange tones. Distinctive quilled flowers appear from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring after frost.

directions: Plant into rich, moisture-retentive soil and stake taller stems in exposed sites. This tall cultivar bears large golden-yellow flowers marked with fine red flecking near the cone. It blooms from midsummer to early autumn and makes a good cut flower.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted stock in spring.

directions: Plant into fertile, damp soil and keep watered during establishment. This cultivar shows warm orange and yellow rays finely streaked with red over a brown cone. Flowering runs from mid to late summer and attracts bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Set out in spring into warm soil.

directions: Plant this tall German cultivar into rich, moist soil and stake if grown in wind. It carries deep coppery mahogany-red flowers around a dark brown central cone. Blooms appear from late summer into autumn on robust stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant into moist, fertile ground toward the front or middle of a border. This early, compact cultivar produces deep bronze-red flowers around a dark cone. It blooms from early summer and repeats when spent flowers are removed.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring.

directions: Set this tall cultivar into rich, damp soil and provide support in windy gardens. The name means gold rush, fitting for its warm golden-yellow flowers flushed with orange. Flowering runs from late summer into autumn and feeds late-season pollinators.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring.

directions: Plant into fertile, moisture-retentive soil and stake taller stems as needed. This cultivar bears warm yellow rays overlaid with soft orange-brown tones around a dark cone. Blooms open from mid to late summer over a long period.

planting.note: Plant in spring into warm soil.

directions: Plant this tall, late cultivar into rich, damp soil and stake in exposed positions. The name means September fox, reflecting fox-red and orange-brown flowers late in the season. It carries bloom well into autumn when many heleniums have finished.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring.

directions: Plant into moist, fertile soil toward the middle of a sunny border. This shorter, bushy cultivar produces masses of bright golden-yellow daisies over a green-yellow cone. Deadhead through the season for continuous bloom from midsummer to early fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost.

directions: Plant this tall heirloom cultivar into rich, damp soil and stake in windy sites. It carries clear lemon-yellow rays around a contrasting dark bronze central cone. Blooms open from late summer into autumn and last well when cut.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter or plant out young stock after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed, as this seed-raised strain comes fairly true from seed. The name means red-gold, describing its mix of yellow, orange, and red-toned flowers. Plants bloom the first or second summer and flower into early autumn.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for first-year bloom.

directions: Surface-sow seed and keep it warm and moist, as this series flowers the first year from an early sowing. Plants produce warm red and mahogany flowers with a golden reverse over a dark cone. Deadhead to extend bloom from midsummer into autumn.

planting.note: Sow indoors early for bloom in the first season.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto moist soil and grow warm for quick first-year flowering. This selection carries bright golden-yellow daisies around a raised brown-gold cone. Plants bloom from midsummer and repeat when deadheaded through the season.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant this compact selection into moist, fertile soil near the border front. Its dwarf, well-branched habit carries bright fiery red flowers over a brown cone. Blooms open from early summer and continue with regular deadheading.

planting.note: Set out potted plants in spring.

directions: Plant this compact series selection into moist, well-drained fertile soil in full sun. Its short, sturdy stems need no staking and bear glowing orange-red flowers. Blooms appear from early summer into autumn and draw many pollinators.

planting.note: Plant in spring into warm soil.

directions: Set this dwarf cultivar into fertile, evenly moist soil toward the border front. Compact and free-branching, it carries warm golden-yellow flowers with hints of orange. It blooms from early summer to autumn without needing support.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost.

directions: Plant this very compact selection into moist, fertile soil at the front of a bed or in a container. Its low mounding habit is topped with orange-and-yellow bicolor flowers. Deadhead for repeat bloom from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring into well-drained soil.

directions: Plant this selection of orange sneezeweed into gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. Formerly grouped with Helenium, it makes a clump of foliage topped by orange-yellow daisies. Blooms open in early to midsummer and suit cooler mountain gardens.

planting.note: Plant in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant into rich, moisture-retentive soil and water well through establishment. This cultivar is grown for its distinctive near-rayless yellow pompom cones on tall stems. The button-like blooms appear from mid to late summer and suit cutting.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring.

directions: Plant into fertile, damp soil and keep the roots from drying during summer. This cultivar carries warm orange-yellow flowers with reddish tones around a brown cone. Bloom runs from mid to late summer and feeds bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring into warm soil.

directions: Set this very tall cultivar into rich, moist soil and stake stems in open sites. The name means garden sun, fitting for its pale yellow rays around a yellow-brown cone. Blooms open from late summer and make a strong back-of-border display.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring.

directions: Plant into fertile, moisture-retentive soil and support taller stems if needed. The name refers to a copper spring, matching its warm copper-orange and yellow flowers. Bloom runs from mid to late summer over dark central cones.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost has passed.

directions: Set this tall cultivar into rich, damp soil and stake in windier gardens. The name means fire seal, reflecting its blend of fiery red and gold on each flower. Bloom opens from late summer and holds into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring into warm soil.

directions: Plant into moist, fertile ground and water through establishment and dry weather. This cultivar carries deep velvety red flowers over a dark brown central cone. It blooms from midsummer into autumn and works well massed in a border.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant this compact selection into moist, fertile soil in full sun near the border front. Its sturdy short stems bear rich red flowers that need no staking. Blooms open from early summer to autumn and attract bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Plant in spring into warm, workable soil.

directions: Plant into rich, evenly moist soil and keep watered while establishing. This cultivar shows multicolored rays streaked and mottled in orange, red, and gold. Blooms run from midsummer into autumn and make lively cut flowers.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring.

directions: Plant into fertile, moisture-retentive soil and stake taller stems if exposed. The name means dark splendor, describing its deep brownish-red flowers over a dark cone. Bloom opens from mid to late summer and lasts well into early autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring after frost.

directions: Set plants into rich, damp soil and water freely during establishment. This cultivar carries warm bicolored flowers blending red, orange, and gold around a brown cone. Blooms open from mid to late summer and draw pollinators to the border.

planting.note: A towering meadow species; site at the back of a border where its height is welcome.

directions: Sow shallowly or plant spring divisions where the soil stays reliably moist. Despite the name the individual daisies are modest, but the plant itself can reach well over head height in a single season. Slender reddish stems carry pale yellow flowers from late summer into fall, a magnet for bees and late butterflies.

planting.note: A woodland-edge sunflower that accepts light shade better than most.

directions: Plant in humus-rich soil at woodland margins where it gets morning sun and afternoon relief. Set divisions with the crown just at the surface and keep them evenly moist the first season. Clear yellow, wide-rayed daisies appear from mid to late summer above thin, rough-textured leaves.

planting.note: Vigorous prairie species; best in large naturalized plantings.

directions: Broadcast or drill seed a quarter inch deep in open ground, or plant spring divisions where the colony can spread. The smooth pale stems and coarsely toothed leaves rise very tall before flowering. Bright yellow daisies crown the plant from late summer, feeding pollinators and finches that later strip the seed.

planting.note: Airy small-flowered species good at the shrubby edge of a garden.

directions: Site in average soil at a partly shaded woodland edge and plant divisions with the crown at grade. Tall wiry stems branch widely into an open, cloud-like form. Dozens of small clear-yellow daisies scatter across the plant in late summer, giving a delicate rather than a bold effect.

planting.note: Compact prairie sunflower with reddish disks; very drought tolerant.

directions: Sow shallowly in lean, well-drained ground or set spring divisions in full sun. This tidy native stands up on its own without staking and asks for little water once rooted. Golden rays surround a distinctive reddish-brown central disk from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Southeastern native with dark central disks and a basal leaf rosette.

directions: Plant in average, well-drained soil in full sun, keeping the leafy rosette at the base uncrowded. Nearly bare flowering stems rise well above the foliage before branching. Yellow daisies centered with a deep red-purple disk open in late summer and fall.

planting.note: Short-statured sunflower with most leaves clustered near the base.

directions: Give this species dry, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun and it stays compact and well behaved. Almost all the foliage sits in a low rosette, so the slender flower stalks read as airy wands. Yellow daisies float above the leaves from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Western native of wet meadows and streamsides; likes moisture.

directions: Plant in moist to wet soil in full sun, ideal along ditches, ponds, or low damp ground. Slender stems and narrow leaves give it a graceful, upright look. Clear yellow daisies bloom from mid to late summer; keep the soil from drying out for best growth.

planting.note: A garden hybrid grown from divisions; parent of many named forms.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in fertile, well-drained soil with the crown level at the surface. This long-cultivated cross between two native sunflowers offers larger, showier daisies than either parent. Golden-yellow flowers cover the clumps from midsummer into early autumn; lift and divide every few years to keep it vigorous.

planting.note: Robust hybrid clump grown from divisions; can spread strongly.

directions: Set divisions in spring in ordinary garden soil and full sun, giving the clump plenty of room to run. It is a vigorous grower that benefits from being lifted and split every second or third year. Bright golden daisies bloom generously from late summer into fall.

planting.note: A widely grown pollinator favorite; propagate by spring division.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and average, well-drained soil, spacing generously for the broad mature clump. Pinch the stems in early summer if you want a shorter, sturdier plant. Soft lemon-yellow daisies with pale centers smother the bush from late summer into fall and draw clouds of bees.

planting.note: Fully double golden form; divide clumps in spring.

directions: Plant spring divisions in rich, moisture-retentive soil in full sun, keeping the crown at grade. Tall stems may need discreet staking under the weight of the flowers. Fully double, pompon-like golden blooms open from late summer and last well as cut flowers.

planting.note: Clear lemon single-flowered form; propagate by division.

directions: Set divisions in spring in fertile soil and full sun, spacing them for good air movement. This form keeps a neater habit than the doubles and rarely needs staking. Star-shaped lemon-yellow daisies bloom over a long stretch from mid to late summer and cut beautifully.

planting.note: Semi-double golden form; grown from spring divisions.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in well-worked soil in full sun and water through dry spells. The semi-double flowers show a ruff of extra petals around a green-gold eye. Rich yellow blooms appear from late summer into early autumn and are excellent for cutting.

planting.note: Dwarf, non-flopping willowleaf selection; divide in spring.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and average soil, setting the crown at the surface. This compact selection keeps the fine willow-like foliage of the species but stays low and self-supporting. A late flush of small golden daisies tops the ferny mound in autumn.

planting.note: Compact willowleaf form that flowers earlier and shorter; divide in spring.

directions: Set divisions in spring in full sun and well-drained soil at grade. Prized for its threadlike foliage, this dwarf selection stays upright without staking. Bright yellow daisies with dark centers cover the plant in early to mid autumn.

planting.note: Very dwarf willowleaf form for the front of the border; divide in spring.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in full sun, spacing them so the low mounds knit together. The threadlike green foliage forms a soft cushion for most of the season. Short-stemmed golden daisies sit close over the mound in autumn, ideal for edging.

planting.note: Soft-yellow Maximilian-type selection; propagate by spring division.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and average to lean soil, giving the tall clump room. This selection carries the pale, creamy-lemon flowers that make it stand out from the brassy species. Blooms line the upper stems in early to mid autumn and are much visited by bees.

planting.note: Large-flowered dark-centered selection; divide in spring.

directions: Set divisions in spring in full sun and average soil, keeping the basal rosette uncrowded. Tall, nearly leafless stems lift the flowers well above the foliage. Broad golden daisies with a striking dark central disk open from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Vigorous large semi-double form; lift and divide regularly.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in ordinary soil and full sun, allowing space for the running clump. Big semi-double golden-orange daisies bloom over a long late-season stretch. It spreads with enthusiasm, so lift and split it every couple of years to keep it in bounds.

planting.note: Old garden selection with warm golden flowers; divide in spring.

directions: Set divisions in spring in average garden soil and full sun with room to spread. This long-cultivated form makes a tall, leafy clump topped with warm golden daisies. Flowering runs from late summer into autumn; divide every few years to renew vigor.

planting.note: Selected swamp sunflower for reliable fall bloom; divide in spring.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and moist soil, since this form thrives where the ground stays damp. Pinch stems in early summer to curb height and improve branching. A dense sheet of gold, dark-centered daisies blankets the plant in mid autumn.

planting.note: Soft-yellow swamp sunflower selection; divide in spring.

directions: Set divisions in spring in full sun and moist to average soil, ideal for a damp border. The paler, buttery flowers blend more easily with other autumn colors than the bright species. Pinch in early summer for a bushier plant, then enjoy a wave of soft yellow daisies in October.

planting.note: Double golden thinleaf selection; propagate by division.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in fertile soil in sun to light shade, keeping the crown at grade. This old double form bears quilled, fully petaled flowers resembling small golden dahlias. Blooms open from mid to late summer and hold up well when cut.

planting.note: Dwarf, floriferous garden selection for smaller borders; divide in spring.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and well-drained soil, spacing for a tidy front-of-border clump. This compact selection stays low and self-supporting while flowering heavily. Bright golden semi-double daisies cover the mound from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for first-year bloom, or direct sow after frost.

directions: Press seed lightly into a firmed surface and cover just enough to hold moisture, keeping it warm until germination. This rough-leaved form is sturdy and upright, thriving in average soil with good drainage and full sun. Golden-yellow single flowers open from early summer onward and draw bees and butterflies.

planting.note: Set out divisions or nursery plants in spring after the ground thaws.

directions: Plant with the crown level with the soil surface and water well to settle the roots. This classic selection carries large bright golden-yellow flowers on strong stems and needs little more than full sun and average soil. Deadhead spent blooms through summer to keep the flush of color going into early fall.

planting.note: Plant in spring where the striking foliage will be seen up close.

directions: Set plants at their original depth in full sun and average, well-drained soil. The ivory leaves veined in dark green are the main draw, topped through summer by single golden-yellow daisies. Trim faded flowers to tidy the plant and encourage a longer bloom season.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in a sunny border for the boldest stem color.

directions: Plant in full sun with the crown at soil level and give it room to reach its tall height. Dark bronze-red stems and foliage set off golden-yellow flowers with a deep red central ring. Cut back after the first flush to promote fresh growth and repeat bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the richest flower coloring.

directions: Set plants level with the surrounding soil in a sunny, well-drained spot. Flowers open deep orange-red and mellow toward gold as they age, giving a two-tone effect over dark-tinted foliage. Remove spent blooms regularly to keep new buds forming through late summer.
